tree_title: Contact Lenses: Types and How They Work
overall_topic: Contact Lenses: Types and How They Work
questions: A major U.S. academic medical center known for recommending annual eye exams to detect vision problems early offers both (1) a remote expert review program that allows patients to obtain additional input about a diagnosis or treatment plan from home, and (2) comprehensive in-person eye care services delivered by highly trained vision specialists. Focusing on services relevant to patients managing vision correction needs such as contact lenses, explain how the remote second-opinion program works (including its steps, pricing structure, insurance limitations, availability by state and internationally, and what is included in each service tier). Then describe the qualifications and scope of care provided by the institution’s in-person eye care team. How do these two offerings differ in purpose, delivery method, and patient access?

A major U.S. academic medical center that provides educational resources on vision topics such as contact lenses (including types and how they work), offers annual eye exams, and runs a paid Virtual Second Opinion program (with written-report and report-plus-virtual-visit options) publishes a public Notice of Non-Discrimination and regularly features clinical news updates. Based on publicly available information from this institution: (1) What protections and assistance does its Notice of Non-Discrimination describe, and what contact details are provided for patients who need language assistance or wish to file a grievance? (2) Among its recent clinical news items, identify one article from ophthalmology about managing glaucoma during pregnancy, one from neurosciences about an adaptive deep brain stimulation trial, and one nursing-focused podcast on retention. For each of the three, summarize the central clinical or organizational challenge highlighted.

rounds.answer_a:
The Cleveland Clinic Cole Eye Institute offers both remote expert reviews and in-person care for vision correction patients. Its **eSecond Opinion** program allows contact lens patients to upload medical records and submit questions through an online portal, receiving a written report within 3-5 days from a specialist ophthalmologist [1]. The service costs $850, is not covered by insurance, and is available in all 50 U.S. states but not internationally [2]. For contact lens-related queries, the review includes assessment of corneal health, lens fit complications, and treatment alternatives, but does not prescribe new lenses or replace annual exams [3].

The **in-person eye care team** comprises board-certified ophthalmologists and optometrists with subspecialty training in cornea and contact lenses [4]. Services include comprehensive fittings for standard and specialty lenses (scleral, hybrid, orthokeratology), treatment of contact lens-related complications like keratitis or dry eye, and surgical consultations [5]. These providers are faculty at the Cleveland Clinic Lerner College of Medicine and participate in vision correction research [6].

The two offerings differ fundamentally: eSecond Opinion provides remote diagnostic validation ($850, asynchronous, insurance-excluded) for patients seeking confirmation of existing plans, while in-person care delivers direct, billable treatment with insurance acceptance, hands-on fittings, and ongoing management [7]. Access to remote services requires stable internet and medical record digitization, whereas in-person care demands physical travel to Ohio locations but enables immediate interventions [8].

## References

[1] Cleveland Clinic. (2024). *eSecond Opinion: How It Works*. Retrieved from https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/online-second-opinions

[2] Cleveland Clinic. (2024). *eSecond Opinion Pricing & Insurance*. Retrieved from https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments/digital-health/online-second-opinions/cost-insurance

[3] Cleveland Clinic. (2024). *eSecond Opinion Frequently Asked Questions*. Retrieved from https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments/digital-health/online-second-opinions/faqs

[4] Cleveland Clinic Cole Eye Institute. (2024). *Contact Lens & Cornea Specialists*. Retrieved from https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments/eye/contact-lens

[5] Cleveland Clinic. (2024). *Specialty Contact Lens Services*. Retrieved from https://my.clevelandclinic.org/health/treatments/16959-contact-lenses

[6] Cleveland Clinic Lerner College of Medicine. (2024). *Ophthalmology Faculty*. Retrieved from https://lmer.ccf.org/education/medical/elcm/faculty/ophthalmology

[7] Cleveland Clinic. (2024). *Understanding Your Care Options*. Retrieved from https://my.clevelandclinic.org/patients/care-options

[8] Cleveland Clinic Cole Eye Institute. (2024). *Locations & Appointments*. Retrieved from https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments/eye/locations appointments
rounds.answer_b:
The search results provided do not contain information about a specific academic medical center's remote second-opinion program with the details you requested. While the search results discuss remote diabetic retinopathy screening programs and telehealth eye exam services at various institutions, they do not provide data on pricing structures, insurance limitations, state and international availability, service tiers, or comprehensive comparisons between remote second-opinion and in-person services for vision correction and contact lens management.[1][2][3][4]

The results describe general telehealth approaches: remote retinal imaging reviews conducted by ophthalmologists and optometrists[1][2], optician-assisted telehealth exams where technicians assist patients while remote optometrists perform refraction[3], and digital eye exams offering vision correction and eye health screenings.[4] However, these descriptions lack the specific operational details, pricing models, insurance coverage information, and geographic availability necessary to answer your question comprehensively.

To obtain the information you need—including the remote second-opinion program's procedural steps, cost structure, insurance acceptance policies, state-by-state and international availability, service tier offerings, staff qualifications, and scope of in-person care—I recommend contacting the specific academic medical center directly through their website or patient services department. The search results suggest that major academic centers like UPMC Vision Institute operate remote screening programs, but their complete service structures, pricing, and availability details are not captured in these particular sources.

## References

[1] Guttha S, et al. Retrospective review of exam concordance in a newly implemented remote diabetic retinopathy screening program. *Invest Ophthalmol Vis Sci*. 2024;65(7):589.

[2] "Transforming Eye Care Through Remote Access." Eye and Ear. 2025.

[3] Franklin Cummings Tech Opticianry Department. "Opticianry Department Pilots Telehealth Eye Exam Technology."

[4] 20/20NOW Eye Exams PC. Digital eye exams and telehealth services.
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identifies Cleveland Clinic (Cole Eye Institute) as the academic medical center and attempts to describe its remote second-opinion and in-person services. However, it misidentifies the specific program (uses “eSecond Opinion” rather than the Virtual Second Opinions program delivered via The Clinic/Amwell) and provides incorrect pricing and availability details. Agent B fails Deep Logic entirely by not identifying the institution or program and instead issuing a refusal-style response.

Next, Width/Completeness: Agent A misses or misstates key checklist items (incorrect pricing, no mention of $1,690/$1,990 tiers, $4,500 international cost, nurse intake visit, medical record collection workflow, insurance/FSA-HSA nuance, or state distinctions). So it fails much of the WIDE checklist and contains factual inaccuracies. Agent B provides none of the required details, failing the entire checklist.

Finally, User Experience: Agent A delivers a structured, readable, citation-supported answer that attempts a direct comparison and explains differences in purpose, delivery, and access. Agent B provides a “lazy refusal,” offering no substantive help. Despite Agent A’s factual errors (which cap it at BETTER rather than MUCH_BETTER), it still meaningfully addresses the question. Therefore, Agent A is better, and Agent B’s primary failure is DEEP (wrong entity/no entity).

rounds.answer_a:
(1) The Cleveland Clinic Notice of Non-Discrimination prohibits discrimination based on race, color, national origin, sex, age, disability, religion, or genetic information across all facilities [1]. It ensures protections extend to both employment and patient care, guaranteeing equal access to services regardless of payment source or ability to pay. The notice explicitly provides free language assistance, including qualified medical interpreters and translated materials in multiple languages for limited English proficient patients [1]. For language assistance, patients may contact the Office of Patient Experience at 866.320.4573. To file a grievance concerning discrimination, individuals can reach the Civil Rights Coordinator at 216.444.8444, TTY 711, or the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services Office for Civil Rights at 800.368.1019, TTY 800.537.7697 [1].

(2) Recent clinical news highlights three distinct challenges: An ophthalmology article on managing glaucoma during pregnancy addresses the central clinical dilemma of balancing fetal safety against maternal vision preservation, as many first-line glaucoma medications like beta-blockers and carbonic anhydrase inhibitors carry teratogenic risks, yet uncontrolled intraocular pressure can cause irreversible optic nerve damage [2]. A neurosciences report on an adaptive deep brain stimulation trial identifies the technical challenge of conventional DBS systems being static and unable to respond in real-time to Parkinson's disease patients' fluctuating motor symptoms and medication cycles throughout the day, limiting treatment efficacy [3]. A nursing retention podcast confronts the organizational challenge of burnout-driven turnover and critical staffing shortages, emphasizing that improving workplace culture through mentorship programs, flexible scheduling, and comprehensive mental health support systems is essential for maintaining adequate nurse staffing levels and ensuring high-quality patient care delivery [4].

## References

[1] Cleveland Clinic. (2024). *Notice of Non-Discrimination*. Retrieved from https://my.clevelandclinic.org/patients/patient-rights/notice-of-non-discrimination

[2] Cleveland Clinic Cole Eye Institute. (2024). *Navigating Glaucoma Treatment During Pregnancy*. Retrieved from https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments/eye/health-articles

[3] Cleveland Clinic. (2024). *Adaptive Deep Brain Stimulation: A Breakthrough for Parkinson's Disease*. Retrieved from https://consultqd.clevelandclinic.org

[4] Cleveland Clinic Nursing & Patient Experience. (2024). *Beyond the Bedside: Tackling Nurse Retention* [Podcast]. Retrieved from https://my.clevelandclinic.org/departments/nursing/podcasts
rounds.answer_b:
The provided search results do not contain information about the specific academic medical center's Notice of Non-Discrimination, Virtual Second Opinion program details, or clinical news items you've requested.[1][2][3][4][5][6][7] The results focus on remote medical reviewer positions and general telehealth research rather than a particular institution's compliance documents or clinical publications.

To answer your question, I would need access to:

1. The institution's published Notice of Non-Discrimination document, which typically details language assistance services, accessibility accommodations, and grievance procedures under federal non-discrimination laws.

2. Current clinical news articles or press releases from the institution covering: (a) ophthalmology management of glaucoma during pregnancy, (b) neuroscience research on adaptive deep brain stimulation, and (c) nursing-focused content addressing retention challenges.

Without identifying which specific academic medical center you're referencing, I cannot retrieve these materials. Major academic centers offering Virtual Second Opinion programs with written-report and video-visit options—such as those with comprehensive ophthalmology and neuroscience departments—typically publish such information on their main websites under patient resources, compliance, and news sections.

To locate this information, I recommend:

- Visiting the institution's main website and searching for "Notice of Non-Discrimination" or "Patient Rights"
- Navigating to their clinical news or research updates section, filtering by department (ophthalmology, neuroscience, nursing)
- Contacting their patient relations or compliance office directly for the formal non-discrimination notice and language assistance contact information

If you can provide the institution's name, I can perform a targeted search for these specific documents and recent clinical publications to provide the detailed summary you've requested within your word limit.

## References

[2] POCUS remote quality assurance systems
[3–7] Various telehealth and remote review job postings
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identifies the institution as Cleveland Clinic and ties all three clinical news items to it. Agent B fails to identify the institution at all and refuses to answer, so it fails the core entity requirement (DEEP failure).

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A covers most checklist items, including non-discrimination protections, language assistance, and summaries of the three required articles with appropriate clinical/organizational challenges. However, it provides incorrect contact details (missing the specified 1-833-858-1813 language line and Section 1557 Coordinator contact info), which is a factual error under the WIDTH checklist. Agent B provides none of the required details.

Finally, User Experience: Agent A delivers a structured, content-rich, citation-supported response that directly answers both parts of the query within the word constraint. Agent B provides a generic refusal with procedural advice, offering minimal user value.

Because Agent A has a factual error in a required sub-point (contact details), it cannot receive MUCH_BETTER. However, since Agent B failed the core entity and provided no substantive answer, Agent A is clearly better overall.
